summary: The current public guarded-Run UX is actionable: an operator can start one command, receive typed stop/recovery information, resume the same Run, read the rendered status, and cancel it. Human input is requested only for a bounded material decision, while PR creation, merge, waiver, and material external effects remain explicit human authority. This is a ux_completion role verdict only; it does not make the stale pr-prepare snapshot PR-ready or approve CI/PR/merge lifecycle actions.
inspection_summary: Inspected the Story, architecture, test plan, current public execute help, action-owner decision boundary, guarded-session recovery/rendering, current dogfood state, and the sole 6d678a1a change (S-004). Re-ran node --test test/e2e/story-vibepro-one-command-pr-ready-closure-runtime.spec.ts at 6d678a1a: 5/5 passed. S-004 drives the public runCli composition through execute run, same-Run resume, human status rendering, and operator cancel, and asserts persisted runtime_unavailable recovery, verification_failed after resume, retry journal, next command, and cancelled transition. S-001 remains a production-shaped real-Git commit/repair/review regression path; S-002 covers the typed-stop and material-human-decision matrix; help and S-005 confirm the authority boundary. The dogfood Run records a typed runtime_unavailable stop with provider, required/missing capability, runtime_available condition, and exact resume command. Regression guard and path-surface coverage both pass: autonomous, legacy/disabled fallback, public JSON/human/status surfaces, persisted state/retry journal, recovery, cancel, and authority exclusions have evidence; no silent suppression or contradictory user-facing output was found.
inspection_evidence: node --test test/e2e/story-vibepro-one-command-pr-ready-closure-runtime.spec.ts (2026-07-24, 5/5 pass at 6d678a1a); node bin/vibepro.js execute --help; .vibepro/executions/story-vibepro-one-command-pr-ready-closure/runs/run-20260723T191944Z-7f564484/state.json; .vibepro/pr/story-vibepro-one-command-pr-ready-closure/pr-prepare.json
judgment_delta: Prior UX evidence was stale at 97c7684; the 6d678a1a S-004 public CLI scenario now directly proves run -> typed runtime stop -> resume -> human status -> cancel at the composition root, so the UX verdict is current for the changed surface.

The review concern that only internal owner tests could prove typed recovery is resolved: S-004 reads the persisted state file and asserts the public human renderer's status, stop code, and resume command, not just an internal return value.

The real dogfood runtime remains unavailable rather than a successful provider execution, but its persisted typed stop is the intended user recovery path. S-001 separately preserves the available-provider production-shaped Git/review/repair regression contract, so this review does not equate runtime unavailability with absence of proof.

Current pr-prepare remains needs_verification and is bound to the previous 97c7684 snapshot; that is an outstanding lifecycle/gate condition, not a UX defect, and this pass does not waive or override it.

regression_guard.evidence: The only production-adjacent delta is a test-only public runCli scenario. It passes with S-001 through S-005 (5/5); S-001 exercises real temporary Git commits, an independent review, repair, and replay on the repaired HEAD, while guarded-run tests cover explicit autonomous-to-legacy fallback and prevent autonomous verification from silently invoking the legacy autopilot.

path_surface_coverage.evidence: Inputs inspected: execute run, resume, status, cancel, material decision, unavailable runtime, verification failure, legacy/disabled fallback, and operator cancellation. Outputs inspected: CLI JSON result, human summary/help, persisted run state, typed stop/recovery, retry journal, transitions, decision/recovery command, Gate-bound final readiness, and the explicit exclusion of PR create/merge/waiver/external effects. S-004 would not pass before its addition because the public composition path did not execute these assertions.
